CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The client requested a full website review for Sound Love Entertainment with a focus on improving user experience, increasing conversions, and boosting online visibility. I completed an in-depth audit of the site and provided recommendations across several key areas, including homepage messaging, navigation, SEO, trust-building, and mobile performance. I identified issues such as a weak homepage call-to-action, lack of service-specific landing pages, limited SEO optimization, and missing trust-building content like testimonials and event photos.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

I recommended strengthening the homepage CTA with more compelling language and

Major restructure and edit of Spark funding proposal, The presentation is now more digestible and compelling, mirroring a slide deck ordering with emphasis on concrete deliverables, traction to date and an end point vision.

Analysis

William has made impressive progress by completing the SBAM classes and submitting a proposal for potential funding opportunities. He has taken initiative by also participating in a housing grant program to learn more about funding options for mixed-use housing projects. With these educational commitments now wrapping up, Will is shifting his focus back to developing his business and building momentum.

Currently, he’s using Shopify as his online store platform to sell T-shirts, but so far, he hasn’t seen any sales come through. This lack of traction could be due to a few areas that need strengthening—such as product presentation, site design, branding, or lack of visibility. Now that he has more time, it’s a great opportunity for him to revamp his approach, especially online. William has the drive and ideas; he just needs a stronger digital presence to support his vision.

Recommendation

I recommend that William conti

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Brad Wahr with Mercantile Bank reached out asking if the RAP grant is assignable to the bank or not. He disclosed the project, so I knew he was talking about this case. I provided him with Joe Frost's contact information for follow up. I discussed this general question with Joe on Tuesday. and he said that it is determined on a case-by-case basis in MEDC's underwriting process. I let Brad know that it's not a definitive no, but it is also not a default yes. Brad indicated he has not yet had time to get back to this project to further assess Mercantiles ability to finance the project.
Lance Wolfe <lwolfe@mtu.edu>

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Sounds like a plan! Thanks so much!

Christine 

Lance Wolfe <lwolfe@mtu.edu>

Keith is working on market research for his food business. In an effort to get feedback, he brought in samples for us to taste test and provide feedback. Based on his previous definition of "elevated ancestral cuisine", the samples fell short and were closer to standard diner fare (the eggs were a bit elevated but too salty).


I recommended that he re-evaluate what he is hoping to do. Perhaps start small with one thing (like breakfast burritos) and perfect those before branching out into full breakfast menu. He is also going to be doing more research, he will be going to the restaurants that he has identified and trying food off of their menu.


He has also been offered a potential position at GM, so he may not be moving forward with his business idea.


I told him to reach out when he is ready to take the next step.

Amy sent an email with her updated budget for final review before submitting to Huntington. I have some questions about her numbers after reviewing so I recommended she schedule a follow up meeting for us to chat.

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Prep time included searching and contacting Lance for the operating agreement template and checking with Joann Roche at MSB on need for operating agreement to close on a loan (while not mentioning client)


Hi Stephanie,


If I recall correctly, you wanted a two-person LLC, If so, you would need to create an operating agreement. I've attached a standard form. There are others that you may use as well. This states who the members are, their rights and responsibilities, etc. There are other forms available as well. You may want to have a lawyer look this over. Your bank will want to see this to close on the loan. 

I've also included the Client Consent form. Fill this out and return to me only if you want Joann at the bank to discuss anything with me. 

Kevin

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The business is in the process of expanding its team and marketing reach. The owner is actively working on hiring a new employee and gathering necessary background information for onboarding. At the same time, they are partnering with Allegra for a direct mail marketing campaign to increase visibility and customer engagement.


As the business grows, ensuring legal protection and hiring the right team are crucial. There is a need to finalize and legally review the employment contract to avoid liability or miscommunication. Simultaneously, the business must ensure the direct mail campaign with Allegra is effectively targeted and aligns with brand messaging.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Legal Review: Send the employee contract to an attorney for review and revision if needed to ensure clarity and compliance.
  • Hiring Process

Worked with Jill and Lynda to plan out their promotion of the completion of study. They want to use it to launch their dog product and its not too late to get that going. Discussion about expanding into Slovakia using a local trainer and vet - should they do a licensing agreement and have them manufacture in Europe, or should they manufacture in US and export to Europe. They will look at the export fees and we will discuss licensing with growth team before they decide.


We meet next week.
setting up meeting end of june to review deck.

Had 20 people come to her open house. Looked at the Facebook ad we did. She got a $1500 booking off of it. Paid .17 per click. She will consider running another one.


We will look at google next time we meet.

Everything has been great. It's very busy and I have no time for anything else yet. I'll get there :) I'm having with the accounting stuff so I'm looking for a CPA since QuickBooks is no help. Thanks for checking in. Hope all is well with you



KC

Referred Victoria to James Shuttleworth as a potential candidate for his uplift program.

Jessica is a multi entrepreneur and has a full time job. She is starting a golf clothing business with a charitable donation from each sale. The clothing is high end specialty shirts and hats. She has an SBA loan and has bee working on getting the business going for several months. She is dealing with issues of suppliers from over seas and distribution in the US. She was able to get an extension on payments for her current SBA loan. She is working on starting another business with a friend... a food card selling mini pancakes. Our sessions are primarily her talking through her ideas and next steps. She has a business plan but it is an organic process for her. She is dedicated to her social impact business with the golf clothing and hopes to be in business in June 2025. She is going to contact me in June for another meeting to review her progress ni getting merchandise from India.

Expansion complete – allowing for better margins

Enhancing awareness is still an issue

Sales are up

There is a general “lull” feeling that is a bit unnatural in the marketplace

  • Savings buying from another company outside of Michigan

Option:

  • Tips to promote GLM
  • Coasters for collaborative marketing with breweries

How can we help:

  • Review and comment on website
    • Order experience

  • Marketing ideas

Reviewed updated slide deck and pitch notes. I liked the changes they made to slides 4 & 5. Cathy practiced her pitch a couple of times. She has part of it memorized but not all of it. I recommended she continue to practice it and reference her slide to remind herself what she is talking about at this point. I also recommended she have her note pages with her, if she needs them.


We discussed some possible questions:

  1. How many employees will you be hiring in 2025? This lead to a conversation on creating job descriptions, applications and being in compliance with required forms, workers compensation, unemployment, I-9 form. I recommended they work with Michigan Works! to assist with job description creation and applications.
  2. What is your net profit? They were not able to answer this question. I recommended they review tab 7b that they submitted as part of their application. We discussed the difference between revenue and net profit. We looked at

Zoom call with Scott and Jeffrey. Discussed product and business model, and key problems product solves and its benefits for users. Discussion focused on better identifying and artivculating the challenges facing buyers, and how to better articulate this in key materials, such as the company's website, sales deck and marketing outreach.

Analysis

Mia has recently made a significant shift in her business direction and is feeling optimistic about the changes ahead. She completed both the Transformation Gems course and the One and All program, which have helped her gain clarity on her next steps. As part of this transformation, she has decided to change her business name to better reflect her new goals. While she's currently waiting for the name change to be finalized so she can begin depositing checks into her account, the excitement for her rebrand is building.

With her renewed focus, Mia has decided to pivot her business model toward affiliate marketing. Rather than selling her own products, she wants to earn revenue through affiliate links and commissions. While she’s new to the affiliate space, she’s eager to learn and just needs guidance on how to apply to the right programs. She’s already made valuable new connections through the Transformation Gems program, whi

CRITICAL PROBLEM: Jesses business was selected for a prmotional video with our SBDC success stories, in a combined video of two Thumb area agribusinesses that have created new ways to make more profit than traditional farming. Contacted him by email to see if he was interested.


Hi Jesse, 


At our regional meeting last Friday, we were discussing which businesses to feature in our professionally made SBDC client videos for Huron and Sanilac County, and we would like to feature your business if that works for you. Since you have worked with us for several years, we'd like to do a video that features you and Szymanski's Creekside Acres in Port Austin in the same video. They are relatively new clients with a Christmas tree farm (but also own Szymanski Seed & Grain), and have a different type of business also featuring cows. Young Grace Szymanski has miniature Highland cows that have been a huge pull to the agritourism aspects of their busines

Talked with the client about business today. The client was lukewarm on completing the impact survey. I spoke with ED about this account this morning. The client asked for some market research reports, which I emailed him. I told him I would send out a survey for him to review.


Email Sent,


Hi Rob, it was nice chatting with you today. I emailed you some Vertical IQ reports earlier. I hope they will be helpful. 

The average carpet and upholstery cleaner operates out of a single location, employs about 5 workers, and generates about $710,000 annually.

  • The carpet and upholstery cleaning industry consists of about 6,900 firms that employ about 37,400 workers and generate about $4.9 billion annually.
  • The industry is fragmented; the top 50 companies account for about 23% of industry revenue.
  • Franchises account for about 19% of the industry. Major franchise operators include Stanley Steamer, Chem-Dry, and ServiceMaster.

Connected with Maxi to discuss what he is currently doing for marketing and offered a few suggestions based on where he is and what is is willing to try.

  • Invite local content creators to visit and review the restaurant (provided 2 suggestions). Saw previous success with this method.
  • Direct social media intern to post on Instagram 1-2x per week showing the space and menu items.
  • Explore working with a social media agency for content creation and account management.
  • Run ads/promotions on Doordash and encourage customers to leave Yelp reviews (consider offering an incentive). These are 2 platforms that are working the best for him so far.

Analysis

Ulrica is currently feeling stuck again in her business journey. After losing out on the $5,000 LEAP grant, she has shifted gears and is now focusing on smaller, more manageable ways to generate income. While it’s frustrating for her to feel like she’s back at square one, she hasn’t given up. She’s taking steps to learn more about fitness and nutrition, which could eventually tie into her business offerings. However, progress has been slow, and she hasn’t fully committed to building out a consistent income stream.

In the past, Ulrica was encouraged to explore digital products as a way to earn passive income, but she hasn’t yet taken action. Given her current focus on fitness and wellness, this could be the perfect time to revisit that idea—especially now that she’s acquiring new skills that could be packaged and shared with others.

Recommendation

I recommend that Rica find a simple digital product idea—like a fitness

I spoke with James McClearen at the Detroit Moves conference at NewLab in Detroit. He was prepping for a panel discussion on drones. New info: 1) expect a big announcement soon from blueflite regarding new partnership, 2) lots of drone industry consolidation going on and expected to continue. Blueflite is in survival mode to wait out the carnage and be one of the survivors, 3) current regulatory environment for beyond visual line of sight (BVLOS) flying is not working, though changes seem to be coming; currently need to get waivers or meet same requirements as an airline, 4) blueflite is purposely an OEM -- designing and making drones to sell to operators; they want to be one step way from operating drones so they don't have to deal with BVLOS applications and paperwork which can take over a year, 5) moving deeper into vertical integration of supply chain to keep costs down and have more control over components specs, goal is to have 80% of parts done in-house, 6) need standard

Client needed help categorizing Wave accounting. Jordan was able to hop on and help. After that the client updated me on her new client count and how things are going. She really just needed to talk through all the work she's been doing. We talked about the "Let Them" theory and not letting others thought/feelings/actions get to her. She needed to get it all out.


We'll meet Monday for a short meeting

Analysis

Dan has made meaningful progress by finally settling on a clear direction for his business. He’s decided to focus on rideshare services while continuing to participate in local housing programs. Although his pace has been a bit slow—he's only attended one housing meeting so far—he has still managed to take key foundational steps. Dan has successfully opened two business bank accounts and secured several credit cards, which puts him in a stronger financial position as he starts to build his operations.

Now that he has a clearer vision, Dan is ready to take the next steps toward building credibility and generating income. While rideshare is his main focus, he’s also open to learning about the medical courier industry, which could provide an additional income stream and complement his transportation business.

Recommendation

Dan should prioritize building a professional online presence by creating a simple, clean web

Analysis

Andrea’s online business is holding steady, though she has noticed a slight slowdown in activity. Despite this, she’s staying motivated and focused, especially as she prepares for her upcoming opportunity at Middle Village. She’s made a clear decision to narrow in on selling sunglasses and has set a bold goal—to become the go-to source for stylish, affordable, and high-quality sunglasses in her market. Her ambition to claim the number one spot is a great mindset that can help her stay focused and energized during the build-up phase.

With Middle Village and other pop-up events ahead, Andrea is in a great position to grow brand recognition locally. Her goal is not just about making sales, but also about standing out visually and creating a memorable brand experience for shoppers.

Recommendation

I recommended that Andrea begin purchasing sunglasses at wholesale prices to maximize profit margins. It’s important
Drew responded, nothing yet. Will reach out when it's a "go"

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I recommend the following:


Woman’s Org: https://www.miwomen.com/connect/women-s-organizations

 

Minority Business Certification: http://www.minoritysupplier.org/membership/mbe-certification/

 

Since pursuing the certification requires time, money and energy on your part, you should first see if your customers see that as an advantage. Since you are the lead salesperson, businesses can 'see' that you are women-owned. Only large companies and government contracting have certain set asides for women-owned businesses and thus need the third party verification. Here is the link to get started: Women-Owned Certification: https://www.greatlakeswbc.org/glwbc-programs/glwbc-programs-certificati

Analysis

Farieda has taken a major step forward in her entrepreneurial journey by securing the $15,000 Enhance Grant, giving her the capital she needs to start building out her salon business. She’s currently in the planning phase, thinking through the details of what she wants her new salon to look and feel like. During our recent conversation, we explored various creative ideas for the space, including design elements, service offerings, and potential customer experiences.

In addition to her Michigan location, Farieda is also contemplating expanding out of state and eventually managing two salon locations. While this vision is exciting and shows her long-term mindset, the immediate focus should be on establishing a strong and successful foundation locally before scaling.

Recommendation

I recommend that Farieda concentrate on building her brand in Michigan first. This includes investing time and energy into designing a stand

CRITICAL PROBLEM: Following up on last month's idea of renting the grainery as an event venue. Hadn't heard back from her on details, so sent the following email.

Hi Lori, 


I suggest you do some research on other event venues in the area to see what the amenities are. The comparisons would be for rustic venues like park pavilions, event barns, local halls. This will give you some data to justify the price since it doesn't have a commercial kitchen or A/C. If you can add A/C, you could probably pay it off in a couple weekend rentals and make it better for your own events. It would be hard to rent as a wedding or open house in July/August without air. Could Howard find a used HVAC unit from a contractor that is upgrading? It doesn't need to be fancy, just workable for short term. If it can be heated as well, it would be even better. 


Did you have more detail to share about square footage, parking spaces, etc for doing a flyer? <

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
HIRING YOUR FIRST EMPLOYEE - A BRIEF OVERVIEW OF INFORMATION


Chengy,


Here is a brief overview of what you need to do when hiring your first employee.

Hiring your first employee is a big step, and there are several legal and administrative tasks to ensure compliance. Here’s a structured guide to help you navigate the process:

  1. Obtain an Employer Identification Number (EIN) – You’ll need an EIN from the IRS for tax reporting.
  2. Set Up Tax Records – Employers must withhold federal and state taxes. This includes:
    • Federal Income Tax (Form W-4)
    • Social Security and Medicare Taxes (FICA)
    • Federal Unemployment Tax (FUTA).
  1. Verify Employee Eligibility

IRS letter shows correct ownership as it registered

Scorp

Form 8822


The IRS does not issue a specific letter that explicitly states business ownership. However, the EIN Confirmation Letter (CP 575) serves as proof of business registration and includes the responsible party's name, which can indicate ownership. If you've misplaced this letter, you can request a replacement 147C Letter from the IRS by calling 1-800-829-4933.

For LLCs, additional documents like the Operating Agreement or Articles of Organization may be required to prove ownership.

Would you like guidance on requesting a 147C Letter or other ownership verification methods?

The client has informed me that she would like to open a wellness center in Idlewild Michigan. She wasn't planning on getting a loan to open the center and thought she would be able to use grants. I informed her I wasn't aware of any grants to start the wellness center. She appeared taken a back and said that wasn't the conversation we had over 1 year ago.


I advised her that I tell clients there are no grants available for business startup. Once I said this the conversation went sideways.


The client wants to rebuild a cabin style wellness center and I advised her that she would need to check with the township to determine if such a development is allowed. Secondly, I'm not aware of any lenders in the area and she would need to ask the township if they know of any lenders who provide loans in the area.


I informed the client that I could help her on the development of her business plan and financi

Rebecca has more time to spend on refining and establishing her SOP/Ops Manual. Closing the Kalsee contract was key to her ability to focus on this element. She is working on adding language to her employee manual and her client contracts. The first item is a "shelter in place" policy for any inclement weather (tornado) such as what hit Portage in May '24. The second is to share language with clients about the holiday schedule and how she'll coordinate if a holiday falls on a day they routinely clean a client's location. I'll send some suggested language by Thursday 5/8. She is also looking to pursue her real estate license to transition over to supporting her husband's business (development) in the next 5 years and sell her cleaning business. Our next steps are to solidify the Ops Manual and all processes so that by year-end, it's all in place. (prep for when she sells the business)

Thanks again for meeting with me recently to talk through your vision for Etna’s BBQ & Seafood at 1381 S Drake Rd. I’m excited to see your food business journey continue, especially with your experience from Titanic Tacos.

Apologies for the delay in following up, I’ve been catching up on messages after being away for a couple of weeks. I appreciate your patience!

As we discussed, completing the business plan template is the best next step, it’ll lay the foundation for your financial projections and help us clearly outline your strategy moving forward. Once we’ve got the plan in place, we’ll work on registering the business and applying for your EIN (Employer Identification Number).

In the meantime, here’s a helpful resource to begin reviewing licensing and inspection requirements through the Kalamazoo County Health & Community Services

The key document is the Buy-Sell Agreement

The employee is purchasing 100% of the stock over an estimated 10-year period for an agreed upon total price.

The purchaser/employee runs the day-to-day operations of the company during the 10-year period at a stipulated salary.

The seller works part-time during the 10-year period for a stipulated salary.

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Divine Connection Event Planning and Consultation

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Analysis

Cathy currently runs an event planning business and is doing everything on her own—from managing logistics to overseeing the actual event. Her business model revolves around planning and executing one large event each year, which requires a great deal of time, energy, and resources. While the event is significant and likely impactful, limiting her work to just one project per year is capping her income and growth potential.

She does have a website, which gives her a professional online presence, but she has expressed no interest in using social media to market her services. She feels she simply doesn’t have the time or energy to manage multiple platforms, especially when she’s handling every aspect of the business herself. While understandable, this also limits her exposure and client reach, making it harder to scale.

Recommendation

To ease the workload and open up new revenue streams, I recommended that she create a

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Rhonda and Ben have not made contact so I sent an email checking to see if they have filed their taxes and to collect impact from their loan renewal.


I will be ready to help with any issues when they reply.

The client is developing a promising solar power distribution technology but faces early-stage challenges typical for many technical founders: identifying the right market, determining product-market fit, and securing funding. They’ve self-funded initial development and now need about $200,000 to move forward. One unique complexity is that their system could benefit multiple overlapping customer types—RV owners, campgrounds, off-grid users, and others—making it unclear which to prioritize. Additionally, they’re still exploring how to position the technology and gain adoption, especially in use cases involving two-step buyers like RV owners and campgrounds. While they have warm contacts and some investor interest, they haven't yet completed deep customer discovery or validated a go-to-market path.


I emphasized the importance of conducting robust customer discovery before building a pitch deck or raising money. I recommended they speak with at least 100 knowledgea

Daniel Yoder <dayoder@mtu.edu>

8:40 AM (0 minutes ago)

to Mark


Hi Mark,


Thanks for your patience in awaiting my reply, as I was out of office last week. 


I think this is a fair arrangement, considering your current scenario. You already seemed to be in "wind-down" mode with your own business to pursue your other interests, instead of actively growing it for a strategic sale. If you can encourage enough of your clients to come on board with Matt, you could eventually get out of your business transition some of what you had hoped. And you don't have to commit to being an employee/subcontractor, either.


You could also hold out to try to find another buyer. However, we have a small business community locally--there isn't a big pool of potential buyers for what you do.


What do you think?




Daniel Yod

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

A woman recently experienced a slip and fall incident and is now facing significant operational and financial challenges.

Sales are currently down 34%, and she is overwhelmed with production demands while lacking sufficient cash reserves. One interested couple is still in talks to purchase the business and is working with a bank, pending an appraisal. Another realtor has expressed interest but is withholding client information. The owner is struggling with equipment issues, including a broken frozen drink machine that is leaking coolant shortly after being repaired.


The business is facing a multifaceted crisis: a major decline in sales, depleted cash reserves, rising production stress, and unresolved equipment failures. The slip and fall is contributing to reduced capacity and decision-maki

Analysis

Kim is in the early stages of building her business and is currently exploring different ways to sell her products. Farmers markets have come up as a strong option, and she’s considering them as a first step to get her products in front of potential customers. However, she’s feeling a bit overwhelmed and unsure of how to get started—especially when it comes to setting up the essentials like a business bank account, credit card processing, and basic bookkeeping.

Her biggest concern right now is figuring out how to successfully sell her products and identify which item will be her main focus. She’s passionate about what she creates but is still trying to pinpoint which of her products will be her signature offering—the one that people will come to know her for. She’s also uncertain about how to create a consistent, professional presentation that will attract customers.

Recommendation

I recommended that Kim focus on dev

Discussed with Bill Mayer of Spark regarding the suitability of i4iq for the BAF software development pilot, and potential resource tradeoffs between Molus vs Bubble third party. Bill was supportive, subject to meeting the strict prerequisite of adequate customer discovery and identifying 10 targets interested in giving feedback from a mockup/mvp and follow on purchase.

Client made the decision to put the business on pause for a moment. Her parents are moving states and she may be as well. She may stay in touch or eventually ask to get transferred if she moves. Undecided for now and closing the account. Will reopen is she reaches out with updates or direction

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Phone call with Jake about making the business case for the bank for his debarker project. Total costs maybe $120,000, annual payments on a 5 year note would be $30K with additional 60-70K in labor cost, so total new cash outlays of $90-100K annually with positive cash of $140-150K from improvements in cleaner waste/chips, less saws/sharpening cost, and less downtime due to waiting on mud on bark to thaw. Coached him on how to present case to bank. Grant application through USDA has been delayed due to administration issues, maybe reopening in June.

ClientIntake.CompanyName: The Fieldhouse Sports Bar and Kitchen, LLC

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Meeting with restaurant specialist and the owner. There are several areas that require attention to improve visibility, operational efficiency, and cost management. These include rising food and labor costs, under-optimized web presence, and a need to leverage promotional opportunities.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Review and reduce food and labor costs to improve profit margins.
  • Optimize the website for SEO and integrate online ordering; identify and implement keywords specific to Port Huron.
  • Look at getting listed on Pure Michigan
  • Prep for America’s Best Restaurants filming in September.
  • Explore composting options for food waste in Genesee County.
  • Consider collaboration with a local culinary school to host a pop-up competition.
  • Maintain car show events on Wednesday nights and budget for DJ costs.

Zoom call wtih Caleb: Discussed pivot to focus less on tech transfer offices and more on corporate innovation. Discussed his pitch and reviewed pitch deck, particularly in preparation for upcoming presentation to a large tech company, suggesting doing a $10K, three month pilot. Session largely focused on reworking language and design of the sales deck and discussing terms of pilot.

Discussed with Bill Mayer of Spark the availability of funding for an incoming ask of $250K first year funding for the MI game development pan-collegiate project that Paul Young is compiling. Bill explained that their substantially smaller entire budget of $150K annual budget is much more than others from across the state, and that other MEDC budgets are in question at the moment, so it's not clear there will be funds available. He suggested looking to corporate sponsorship, foundation grants and other revenue streams as alternatives. Paul Young had suggested a couple months window for securing funding, which doesn't look realistic from any state sources, more likely by the end of the fiscal year, or thereafter.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Site visit with contractor and restaurant specialist to look at the kitchen set up and needs.





RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

Connie is going to work on getting an equipment list together and her and I will work on projections. Emily to review drawing.





NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:

Connie to work on numbers





IMPACT REMINDER: client is aware

Financial forecasting session with client. Introduced client to the DIY template and worked with her to start modeling their commercialization strategy. Additional prep time for customizations to client's financial model after the session.

It was a pleasure meeting with you and discussing your business. Here are the resources I mentioned. 


ChatGPT/AI. These tools are helpful for idea generation. However, the business plan is not every conceivable ideal customer/target market and revenue stream that one could implement. I recommend that you look at no more than 1 target market  (see below) and only a few specific revenue models that you will implement on day one. Other ideas are great, but should be put in a section about expansion. 


Cash Flow By Month: https://michigansbdc.org/wp-content/uploads/

I spoke with our client by telephone. She said that business is down quite a bit with the golf courses opened up and the weather getting better. When we last spoke we touched on several things:

  • They are still considering dropping the axe throwing part due to the high liability insurance costs. They haven't dropped in yet and are looking for another provider to help lower the cost
  • We had talked about using AI to generate marketing content. The lady that does their marketing has tried it with positive results. I encouraged her to continue with that
  • She has been pursuing a liquor license and is close to getting approved. Cost would be $20K to purchase from the state. There is a local person selling one for $100K. She mentioned that outside the city she could purchase one for $600 which doesn't quite make sense

Though they haven't realized the sales they had anticipated they are not ready to give up on their business. I reminde

Initial discussion, delineated trade offs between existing on prem solution using Raspberry Pi and cloud deployment.

hipaa compliance considerations, and emphasized the tight competitive landscape in the transcription space. He will send me the competition research he's already done, and I'll help figure out if billing code handling can be a product differentiation.

Although it's possible that he'll be moving out of Ann Arbor in two months at the end of his lease, it will be good to connect him with Spark to integrate into the startup ecosystem now, as he is a first time entrepreneur.

Follow up meeting with Amy to review her financials. We edited her variable expenses and talked through the process with Huntington from here. I also recommended they show a gradual sales increase year to year so we budgeted a 10% increase year 1 to 2. They plan on being mobile for the first two years so there will be no need to hire and they will have very little overhead. Loan request is $13k. They will keep me posted how things go with Huntington.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I called our client to see how she was doing on her HUB grant submission. We looked at what she had written and discussed in what direction she should go. She will be going back to work soon and won't have as much time to spend on other projects. She is adding two foster children next week that are younger siblings of children they currently care for. (Could you be any busier?)

We discussed:

  • Trying to make the most of her social media platforms
  • She is working with Angie Evans for possible radio ad campaign. Angie also suggests other media resources and has worked with our client on community projects
  • Her revenues have increased recently to the point she doesn't have to make a personal deposit to cover expenses this month

I encouraged her to send me her P&L for last year and to date this year for the spreadsheet for projects for the grant app.

Richard returned my call, he ended up closing the business in 2022. He was unable to get the PPP and did not have enough revenue to stay open. He is interested in reopening the business but needs funding. We talked about business loans but when we got to business plans he said he didn't want to write one because he has been doing business for so many years and cannot see why he needs to provide the bank with a business plan. He also expressed fear that his credit score was too low to work with the bank. I explained why banks request a business plan and financial projections and offered to help him with both. I also mentioned GROW, NI and OppFund as lending options if he's denied at the bank. We walked through the business plan outline and I sent via email. He will work on the draft and send to me when he's ready for me to review. I put a follow up on my calendar to call him in two weeks if I don't hear from him.

I spoke with our client. Last time he said that he had put his plans on hold. That hasn't changed and he hadn't thought much about it. I said that I would close out his account with us and that he could reach out to us when he needed assistance.

Daniel Yoder <dayoder@mtu.edu>

4:49 PM (0 minutes ago)

to Robert


Bob,


Your narrative has come a long way, and you have touched on all the major components of the written portion of your plan. I think our focus should now shift to the financials.


Financials forecast thoughts and feedback:

Revenue - You don't show revenue until 2027, which will be a hard pill for a lender to swallow. Is there any possibility of generating revenue in 2026? A lender will want to know that you can at least pay the interest on loans during a "ramp up" period.

Direct costs - it appears that all the costs you have listed here are property improvement and equipment costs. These need to be moved to the "Assets" tab. The assets tab is to document large, one-time purchases that have a long useful life.

Financing - Loans are usually grouped to match the us

Jade Bliss, Inc – sells natural, holistic remedies jadebliss.com

For the short term:

Ø Unemployment/healthcare/managing costs and subscriptions

Analysis

William has completed all of his previous business development classes and has now enrolled in a new program called Thrive, which is part of Operation Hope and connected with LEDC and LEAP through the SWAG initiative. This new class marks a continued commitment to learning and growing his business. In addition to his coursework, Will is actively finding creative ways to get involved in the community and promote his brand.

Although he missed the official deadline to be a vendor for Pride Month, William didn’t let that stop him. He reached out to Sir Pizza to see if he could still participate in some way. Thanks to his initiative, they agreed to let him set up in their parking lot or near the entrance. This resourcefulness and willingness to think outside the box are great qualities that will serve him well as an entrepreneur.

Recommendation

Since William is setting up as a vendor for a high-traffic community

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Chloe has started a draft of her plan and life plan. I reviewed. The plan appears to be well thought out.

The plan could benefit from some additional detail reference to the owner's operational experience, a comprehensive day-to-day operational plan, a more detailed marketing budget aligned to the specific segments. Additional information will be needed on the site analysis including permitting some thought will need to be given to a contingency plan. A bit of clarity could be added to the organizational chart and staffing roles.


At this point, some of the biggest risks are the financial risk of a million dollar SBA backed financing. It is unclear if the client has the 10 to 30 percent that will be needed from an owner injection standpoint. The timeline seems a bit aggressive from a construction and build-out standpoint. Revenue projections seem fairly aggressive based on unknown market adoption.
Dennis emailed with questions regarding financial benchmarks for the industry. I pulled a vertical IQ report for Wineries and emailed to him.

Case prep: review of client intake documents.


case notes:


Nancy is a glass and watercolor artist as a hobby. She works full-time at Munson Hospital in Charlotte boy. She is interested in selling via Etsy and other online platforms as well as galleries. She does not want to engage in craft fairs or any type of pop-up event. She has a studio in her basement where she has some assets, such as a kiln which she uses for her glass work.


We discussed various ways for her to form her entity, including DBA or LLC. It appears that a DBA may work best for her at this point. We discussed the process for registering her DBA through the county.


We also discussed sales and use tax for the state of Michigan. She was provided links to the state website. Additionally, we discussed using platforms like Etsy and the collection and payment of sales and use tax.


She also has a desire to set up a new website And intends

Dallen has made good progress since he last spoke with Shawn, saying that his 2024 sales were ~$900k and that he expects to do $1 - $1.2M this year. He seems to be generating good organic growth and he's going to make a push into wholesale this year, offering journals to small independent retailers. We discussed doing more outreach from his website, building up his newsletter, promoting Journaling in various ways, getting his products to Journaling influencers, etc.


This business seems sound operationally, it's just him and various vendors and agencies. We discussed getting an LOC in the business' name so that he isn't using his HELOC so much.


His main concern is tariff uncertainty. He currently sources his products from China and is looking at Vietnam as a replacement. We discussed domestic options (recycled, hemp, bamboo, etc.) but nothing seems financially viable at this time. We also discussed print-on-demand, which he

Sierra is developing a marketplace app to connect small businesses with local gig workers, and she’s currently navigating several foundational startup challenges. These include completing the proper tech company registration, building a reliable financial forecast, and preparing for a pitch at Michigan Tech Week. She’s working through key assumptions like average gig value, conversion rates for premium subscriptions, and the frequency of gig placements by small businesses. Sierra is also balancing limited funding with plans to hire, monetize through subscriptions, and expand geographically—all while validating product-market fit.


I guided Sierra through the company registration process and provided her with a structured Google Sheets template for financial forecasting. I recommended grounding her projections in real-world data by validating her gig value and subscription rate assumptions. We also reviewed cost structures, including Stripe fees, hosting costs, and Ap

Hi!


I just checked if Pamper Pod LLC was available and it is. You can also check for yourself: https://cofs.lara.state.mi.us/SearchApi/Search/Search

Great question! You do not need to fill out all the boxes or add more articles unless you have something very specific you want to include. Here's a quick breakdown of what to put:

  • Article I – Name:
  • Just enter the name of your LLC exactly how you want it to appear (make sure it ends with “LLC”).
  • Article II – Purpose:
  • You can leave this blank unless you want to write a more specific purpose for your business. Most people leave it blank, and it defaults to “any legal business activity.”
  • Article III – Duration:
  • Leave this blank unless your business is temporary. If you plan to keep it

B - App for adult learners to graduate faster. Michigan programs that may be competition or partnerships: Reconnect (25+ with no degree can go to Community College free) and 65/30 (65% graduation by 2030). Problem is throwing money as trying to get adults to complete school, but there's a larger problem that is not being addressed in that adult learners tend to not graduate and don't have enough peer support. Hi Teca,

I hope you're doing well! I wanted to follow up on our recent meeting and apologize for the delay in getting back to you—I’ve been catching up on emails after being away for a couple of weeks. Thanks so much for your patience.

It was great learning more about your vision for the laser cleaning business. I think it’s a strong concept, especially with the B2B focus and the relationships you’ve already built. As we discussed, the next step is strengthening your business plan—specifically by adding your estimated monthly expenses. That will help us prepare solid financial projections to share with potential lenders or investors, especially as you get ready to reconnect with Kalamazoo Forward Ventures.

Once we get the business plan finalized, we can also move forward with registering your business and applying for an EIN, if you haven’t done that yet.

Let me know how things are going so far, and feel free to book a time with me if you’d like to walk t

Analysis

Angie is currently running her home health care business successfully as an independent contractor. She has established a solid foundation in her current field and is now ready to take the next step—expanding into housing. Recognizing the growing need for supportive housing options, especially for seniors or individuals needing care, Angie is positioning herself to bridge that gap by learning the ins and outs of the housing industry.

She has started attending housing meetings to become familiar with the funding processes, development options, and the resources available for individuals interested in creating housing solutions. Her goal is not just to own property, but to build or secure housing that can support her long-term vision—possibly blending it with her health care services for a more integrated approach.

Recommendation

I recommend that Angie continue attending the Housing Trust Fund meetings and other releva

Analysis

Reggie is actively working to rebuild his lawn care business after experiencing a major setback—his equipment was stolen about a year ago. Since then, he’s been working hard to recover financially and get his business back on track. Right now, he’s taking on freelance jobs to stay afloat, but he’s facing challenges due to having to rent equipment for each job, which cuts into his profits. Despite the hardship, Reg remains committed and is showing resilience by continuing to take on work and move forward, even under pressure.

He’s in a place where he needs support—not only financially but also in terms of knowledge, structure, and long-term planning. He’s doing what he can with what he has, and now it’s time to help him build a stronger foundation so that he can move from survival to stability.

Recommendation

I recommend that Reggie continue to take on as much freelance work as possible to keep some income flowing, bu

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Analysis

Derrick is currently generating about $3,000 per month in product sales. While that’s a solid starting point, growth has plateaued. The market has become significantly more competitive compared to three years ago, and he’s finding it harder to stand out. Right now, he’s in the process of adjusting to new technologies—particularly AI—but hasn’t yet implemented any strategies that are moving the needle. His current methods aren’t yielding increased sales, and without a new approach, there’s a risk of falling further behind in a saturated market.

He has the product and the motivation but lacks a diversified or updated strategy that aligns with today’s consumer behavior and digital trends. The challenge is no longer just about having a good product; it’s about getting the right product in front of the right audience—at the right time and with the right message.

Recommendation

To remain competitive, he needs to evolve his

Went over proforma again. They are over projecting and have a lot of hurdles, especially the lack of a liquor license and no experience running a business. The plan is still convoluted and I can not convince them that they will not be profitable unless they figure out the liquor license problem.

Hi Megan and Jordan,


I can help you with the proforma. If you can send me the financials from the seller, I can get started and then we can set up a time to discuss and make changes to best fit your vision going forward. If you can get them over to me by the end of the day, we could meet sometime Friday if that works. 


Unfortunately I have a conference next week that will limit my availability, but I do have time on Monday as well if that works better.
